codeSnapshot.label: data_augmentation.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torchvision.transforms as T
from torchvision.transforms import v2 as T2  # modern API
import numpy as np

# Standard image augmentation pipeline (ImageNet training)
train_transform = T.Compose([
    T.RandomResizedCrop(224, scale=(0.08, 1.0)),   # crop + scale
    T.RandomHorizontalFlip(p=0.5),                 # flip
    T.ColorJitter(brightness=0.4, contrast=0.4,    # colour
                  saturation=0.4, hue=0.1),
    T.ToTensor(),
    T.Normalize(mean=[0.485, 0.456, 0.406],
                std=[0.229, 0.224, 0.225]),         # ImageNet stats
])

# MixUp augmentation
def mixup(x, y, alpha=0.2):
    """x: (B,C,H,W), y: (B,) class labels"""
    lam = np.random.beta(alpha, alpha)
    idx = torch.randperm(x.size(0))
    mixed_x = lam * x + (1 - lam) * x[idx]
    # Mixed labels (soft): return both y and y[idx] with lam
    return mixed_x, y, y[idx], lam

# CutMix augmentation
def cutmix(x, y, alpha=1.0):
    lam = np.random.beta(alpha, alpha)
    B, C, H, W = x.shape
    cut_ratio = np.sqrt(1.0 - lam)
    cut_h, cut_w = int(H * cut_ratio), int(W * cut_ratio)
    cx = np.random.randint(W); cy = np.random.randint(H)
    x1 = np.clip(cx - cut_w // 2, 0, W); x2 = np.clip(cx + cut_w // 2, 0, W)
    y1 = np.clip(cy - cut_h // 2, 0, H); y2 = np.clip(cy + cut_h // 2, 0, H)
    idx = torch.randperm(B)
    mixed_x = x.clone()
    mixed_x[:, :, y1:y2, x1:x2] = x[idx, :, y1:y2, x1:x2]
    lam = 1 - (x2 - x1) * (y2 - y1) / (W * H)
    return mixed_x, y, y[idx], lam

# RandAugment (used in ViT, EfficientNet)
train_transform_rand = T.Compose([
    T.RandomResizedCrop(224),
    T.RandAugment(num_ops=2, magnitude=9),  # 2 random aug ops, strength 9
    T.ToTensor(),
    T.Normalize([0.485,0.456,0.406],[0.229,0.224,0.225]),
])

eli3: Data augmentation creates new training examples by applying random transformations to existing ones. Flip an image horizontally, randomly crop it, change its colours a little — the object is still recognisable, but the model sees it in a slightly different form. This teaches the model to be invariant to these irrelevant changes.

More training data means better generalisation. When you can't collect more real data, augmentation creates artificial diversity. A model trained on cats at every angle, lighting, and crop is more robust than one trained only on centred, well-lit cats.

Modern techniques like MixUp and CutMix mix two training examples together — the model must simultaneously recognise both classes in a blended image or predict from a patch-swapped image. These harder, artificial examples prevent overfitting more effectively than simple geometric transforms alone.

whenWhy.title: Image classification training (essential, not optional)
whenWhy.body: Without augmentation, ImageNet ResNet-50 achieves ~73% top-1. With standard augmentation (random crop, horizontal flip, color jitter): ~76%. With MixUp + CutMix + RandAugment: ~80%+. Augmentation is the primary tool for preventing overfitting in vision — it often provides more benefit than architecture changes. Every ImageNet training recipe uses it.

whenWhy.title: Limited data regimes (medical imaging, satellite data)
whenWhy.body: When datasets have only 100–1000 examples per class, augmentation is critical for survival. Domain-specific augmentations: for medical imaging, elastic deformations and random rotations; for satellite imagery, random flips and colour shifts. Strong augmentation can improve performance by 10–20% on small medical datasets where collecting more data is expensive or impossible.

whenWhy.title: Self-supervised learning (SimCLR, DINO, MAE)
whenWhy.body: Self-supervised methods create positive pairs by applying two different random augmentations to the same image — the model must learn representations invariant to these augmentations. SimCLR's augmentation policy (random crop, colour jitter, Gaussian blur, grayscale) was carefully tuned to maximise the quality of learned features. The choice of augmentation directly determines what invariances the model learns.

deepDiveIntro: The standard image augmentation pipeline uses random resized crops (which vary both scale and position), random horizontal flips, and colour jitter (brightness, contrast, saturation). Together these teach the model to be invariant to scale, position, orientation, and lighting changes. The random crop is the single most impactful augmentation — by itself it accounts for most of the regularisation benefit. [1]

MixUp blends two training images by linearly interpolating both the pixel values and the class labels using a weight drawn randomly from a Beta distribution. Training with these blended labels forces the model to produce smooth, interpolated predictions between classes, which regularises the decision boundary and improves probability calibration. CutMix provides a complementary benefit by swapping rectangular patches between images rather than blending them globally. [2]

deepDive.heading: Standard Pipeline: Random Crop + Flip
deepDive.body: A random resized crop selects a portion of the image at a random scale (8% to 100% of the area) and random aspect ratio, then resizes to the target size. This creates enormous diversity — the model sees the same object at different scales and positions in every epoch. It is substantially more powerful than simply cropping the centre of each image. Random horizontal flip is applied with 50% probability for symmetric object classes, but vertical flip should only be used when the task genuinely allows upside-down images (such as satellite imagery). [1]

deepDive.heading: MixUp and CutMix
deepDive.body: MixUp creates a blended image by linearly combining two images, and mixes their labels in the same proportion. The model is trained to output probabilities proportional to how much of each class is present. CutMix pastes a rectangular crop from one image onto another and adjusts the label proportionally to the patch area. CutMix teaches the model to classify from local patches rather than global context, which is complementary to MixUp's global blending. Both use a randomly drawn mixing coefficient and are commonly combined in transformer training recipes. [2]
deepDive.formula: MixUp: x̃ = λxᵢ+(1−λ)xⱼ, ỹ = λyᵢ+(1−λ)yⱼ, λ∼Beta(α,α)

deepDive.heading: RandAugment
deepDive.body: RandAugment applies a fixed number of randomly selected transformations (shear, rotate, colour, brightness, contrast, solarize, equalize, etc.) each at the same global magnitude level. Using only two hyperparameters — number of operations and magnitude — it avoids the expensive combinatorial search required by AutoAugment while achieving similar or better performance. N=2 and M=9 are the standard defaults for ViT and EfficientNet training. [1]

deepDive.heading: NLP and Tabular Augmentation
deepDive.body: For text, back-translation (translate to another language and back) paraphrases sentences while preserving meaning, and synonym replacement swaps content words with synonyms. Token masking used in BERT pre-training is itself a form of augmentation. For tabular data, SMOTE generates synthetic minority-class examples by interpolating between existing ones to address class imbalance, while Gaussian noise on continuous features and random feature dropout provide additional regularisation. [1]

youtubeLabel: Data Augmentation in Deep Learning — PyTorch
takeaways: Random crop + flip + colour jitter: adds scale, position, flip, illumination invariance. [1]
MixUp: x̃=λxᵢ+(1−λ)xⱼ, ỹ=λyᵢ+(1−λ)yⱼ — smooth decision boundaries, better calibration. [2]
CutMix: patch swap between images — local feature robustness, complementary to MixUp. [2]
RandAugment(N=2, M=9): N random ops at magnitude M — avoids combinatorial search. [1]
SimCLR/DINO: augmentation defines invariances in self-supervised learning. [1]

edgeCases.q: Which augmentations should NOT be used and why?
edgeCases.a: Vertical flip: only valid if the task is rotationally symmetric (satellite imagery). For natural images (cats, faces, vehicles), vertical flip produces unrealistic examples that may confuse the model. Random colour inversion (negation): produces unrealistic examples for colour-sensitive tasks. Strong augmentations (random erasing, cutout) can hurt performance if the erased region contains the discriminative feature — the model learns to predict without seeing the critical part. For fine-grained recognition (bird species), aggressive cropping may remove the key discriminative features (beak, wing pattern). Always validate augmentation effectiveness on a validation set.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle augmentation for test-time improvement (TTA)?
edgeCases.a: Test-Time Augmentation (TTA): apply multiple random augmentations to the same test image, get K predictions, average them. This approximates an ensemble of models with slightly different inputs. Standard TTA for image classification: 5–10 crops (4 corners + center) × horizontal flip = 10 predictions per image. TTA typically improves top-1 accuracy by 0.5–2% with K=10. Trade-off: K× inference cost. TTA is common in competition settings but rare in production due to latency constraints. For ViTs and ConvNeXt, TTA with centre crop vs multi-crop shows different quality-latency tradeoffs.

sources.label: Shorten & Khoshgoftaar (2019) — A survey on Image Data Augmentation for Deep Learning, J. Big Data 6:60
Zhang et al. (2018) — mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization arXiv:1710.09412
